title: The cost of fluency
summary: Becoming 'native-like' in a second language is sold as pure gain. The essay argues that something specific is lost on the way — and that fluent speakers no longer notice they have lost it.
body:
Most discussions about learning a second language assume that perfect fluency is the obvious goal. Advertisements promise it. Textbooks promise it. We are told that the only thing standing between a learner and 'native-like' speech is enough hours, enough effort, enough courage. What is rarely admitted is that arriving at fluency has a cost — and that some of what is lost cannot be retrieved later.

Consider what happens when a language stops feeling foreign. A learner who has just begun French finds the word 'fenêtre' beautiful, slightly absurd, the way a child finds a new word. By the time fluency arrives, 'fenêtre' is just 'window', and the strangeness is gone. The word has become transparent. We see through it to the thing, instead of seeing it. Most fluent speakers never notice this loss; they have nothing to compare it to.

There is a second, quieter loss. People speaking a second language often produce sentences they would never produce in their first. A learner of English may say, slowly: 'The rain — it walks across the city like an old man.' A native speaker, asked to describe the rain, will rarely reach for that image. They have learned what is and is not 'normal' to say, and the slight awkwardness that produced the original metaphor has been smoothed away. Fluency, in this respect, is a kind of polite censorship: it teaches you what people in this language do not, on the whole, say.

This is not an argument against learning. The benefits are obvious — speaking the language well opens entire countries of people to you, and a great deal of literature, and a portion of yourself that did not exist before. But it is worth remembering that competence costs something. Every native speaker is, in a sense, a former learner who has stopped noticing.

What can we do about this? Perhaps less than we wish. One useful habit is to keep a small notebook of phrases that still feel strange. Write them down before fluency erases them. They are, in a way, the gifts that being bad at a language gives you, and once you are good, no one will ever give them to you again.

It is also worth talking, sometimes, to a beginner. Not to correct them — to listen. Their accidental, awkward sentences are, occasionally, the most accurate description of the world you will hear that month.

annotations.meaningVi: trong suốt; ở đây: từ ngữ trở nên 'vô hình', ta nhìn xuyên qua nó tới sự vật
sự kiểm duyệt (ở đây dùng theo nghĩa ẩn dụ)

grammarNotes.title: Cleft sentences with 'What … is …' for emphasis
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu chẻ (cleft) với cấu trúc 'What X is/has Y' dùng để nhấn mạnh phần thông tin đứng sau 'is' hoặc 'has'. Trong bài: 'What is rarely admitted is that arriving at fluency has a cost.' (Điều ít được thừa nhận là sự lưu loát có cái giá của nó.) Một câu đơn giản hơn — 'People rarely admit that…' — mất đi sự nhấn mạnh. Cấu trúc câu chẻ đẩy thông tin then chốt ra sau và làm nó nổi bật.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Cleft sentences with 'What X is/has Y' put emphasis on the information after 'is' or 'has'. From the essay: 'What is rarely admitted is that arriving at fluency has a cost.' Compare a flatter version — 'People rarely admit that…' — which lacks the same weight. The cleft structure delays and highlights the key claim.

cloze.template:
Most discussions about learning a second language assume that perfect {{b1}} is the obvious goal. Advertisements promise it. Textbooks promise it. We are told that the only thing standing between a learner and 'native-like' speech is enough hours, enough effort, enough {{b2}}. What is rarely admitted is that arriving at fluency has a cost — and that some of what is lost cannot be {{b3}} later.

Consider what happens when a language stops feeling {{b4}}. A learner who has just begun French finds the word 'fenêtre' beautiful, slightly {{b5}}, the way a child finds a new word. By the time fluency arrives, 'fenêtre' is just 'window', and the strangeness is gone. The word has become {{b6}}. We see through it to the thing, instead of seeing it. Most fluent speakers never notice this loss; they have nothing to compare it to.

There is a second, quieter loss. People speaking a second language often {{b7}} sentences they would never produce in their first. A learner of English may say, slowly: 'The rain — it walks across the city like an old man.' A native speaker, asked to describe the rain, will rarely reach for that image. They have learned what is and is not 'normal' to say, and the slight awkwardness that produced the original {{b8}} has been smoothed away. Fluency, in this respect, is a kind of polite {{b9}}: it teaches you what people in this language do not, on the whole, say.

This is not an argument against learning. The benefits are obvious — speaking the language well opens entire countries of people to you, and a great deal of literature, and a portion of yourself that did not exist before. But it is worth remembering that {{b10}} costs something. Every native speaker is, in a sense, a {{b11}} learner who has stopped noticing.

What can we do about this? Perhaps less than we wish. One useful habit is to keep a small {{b12}} of phrases that still feel strange. Write them down before fluency {{b13}} them. They are, in a way, the {{b14}} that being bad at a language gives you, and once you are good, no one will ever give them to you again.

It is also worth talking, sometimes, to a {{b15}}. Not to correct them — to listen. Their accidental, awkward sentences are, occasionally, the most {{b16}} description of the world you will hear that month.

blanks.explanation: 'Perfect fluency is the obvious goal' — the assumption the essay then questions.
The triplet 'enough hours, enough effort, enough courage' completes a rhythm.
Some of what is lost cannot be retrieved (got back) later.
When a language stops feeling foreign — that is, when it becomes ordinary.
A learner finds the word 'beautiful, slightly absurd' — both charming and strange.
The word has become transparent — we look through it to the object, no longer seeing the word itself.
Learners often produce sentences they would never produce in their first language.
The original metaphor — the rain that 'walks across the city like an old man' — gets smoothed away by fluency.
Polite censorship — fluency teaches you what is not normally said, quietly removing options.
Competence costs something — being good at the language is not a pure gain.
Every native speaker is a 'former learner who has stopped noticing' — they were once where the learner is now.
A small notebook of phrases that still feel strange — a personal record.
Write them down before fluency erases them — fluency removes the strangeness without warning.
'The gifts that being bad at a language gives you' — the unexpected creative phrases of a learner.
Talk to a beginner — listen to the accidental, awkward sentences they make.
'The most accurate description of the world you will hear that month' — sometimes a beginner sees something a native speaker cannot say.

translationVi:
Phần lớn các cuộc thảo luận về việc học ngoại ngữ đều mặc định rằng sự lưu loát hoàn hảo là mục tiêu hiển nhiên. Quảng cáo hứa hẹn điều đó. Sách giáo khoa hứa hẹn điều đó. Chúng ta được nghe rằng cái duy nhất ngăn cách một người học với cách nói 'như người bản xứ' là đủ giờ, đủ nỗ lực, đủ can đảm. Điều ít được thừa nhận là sự lưu loát có cái giá của nó — và một phần những gì bị mất sẽ không lấy lại được sau này.

Hãy nghĩ về điều gì xảy ra khi một ngôn ngữ thôi cảm thấy xa lạ. Một người vừa bắt đầu học tiếng Pháp thấy từ 'fenêtre' đẹp, hơi vô lý, giống như cách một đứa trẻ phát hiện một từ mới. Đến lúc đạt được sự lưu loát, 'fenêtre' chỉ còn là 'cửa sổ', và sự lạ lùng biến mất. Từ ấy đã trở nên trong suốt. Chúng ta nhìn xuyên qua nó tới đồ vật, thay vì nhìn vào chính từ. Hầu hết người nói lưu loát không bao giờ nhận ra mất mát này; họ không có gì để so sánh.

Có một mất mát thứ hai, lặng lẽ hơn. Người nói một ngôn ngữ thứ hai thường tạo ra những câu mà họ sẽ không bao giờ tạo ra trong tiếng mẹ đẻ. Một người học tiếng Anh có thể nói chậm rãi: 'The rain — it walks across the city like an old man.' (Cơn mưa — nó đi qua thành phố như một ông già.) Một người bản xứ, khi được yêu cầu mô tả cơn mưa, hiếm khi với tới hình ảnh đó. Họ đã học được điều gì là 'bình thường' và điều gì không, và sự lúng túng nhẹ tạo ra phép ẩn dụ ban đầu đã bị mài nhẵn đi. Ở khía cạnh này, sự lưu loát là một dạng kiểm duyệt lịch sự: nó dạy bạn những điều mà trong tổng thể người ta không hay nói trong ngôn ngữ ấy.

Đây không phải là lời lập luận chống lại việc học. Lợi ích rất rõ — nói được ngôn ngữ ấy mở ra cho bạn cả những quốc gia con người, một khối lượng văn học khổng lồ, và một phần của chính bạn mà trước đó chưa từng tồn tại. Nhưng cũng cần nhớ rằng năng lực có cái giá của nó. Theo nghĩa nào đó, mỗi người bản xứ là một người học cũ đã thôi không còn để ý nữa.

Chúng ta có thể làm gì? Có lẽ ít hơn ta mong. Một thói quen hữu ích là giữ một cuốn sổ nhỏ ghi lại những cụm từ vẫn còn cảm giác kỳ lạ với mình. Hãy viết chúng xuống trước khi sự lưu loát xóa chúng đi. Đó là, theo một cách nào đó, những món quà mà việc dở ngoại ngữ ban cho bạn, và một khi bạn đã giỏi, không ai sẽ tặng lại cho bạn được nữa.

Đôi khi cũng nên trò chuyện với một người mới bắt đầu. Không phải để sửa họ — để lắng nghe. Những câu vụng về, tình cờ của họ đôi khi là mô tả chính xác nhất về thế giới mà bạn sẽ nghe được trong cả tháng.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say is rarely admitted in discussions of fluency?
options: That it is impossible to achieve
That arriving at fluency has a cost, and some of what is lost cannot be retrieved
That courage matters more than effort
questions.explanation: 'What is rarely admitted is that arriving at fluency has a cost — and that some of what is lost cannot be retrieved later.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 1.

questions.prompt: What happens to the word 'fenêtre' once a French learner becomes fluent?
options: It becomes transparent — the strangeness goes, and the word is just 'window'
questions.explanation: '"Fenêtre" is just "window", and the strangeness is gone. The word has become transparent.'

questions.prompt: Why don't fluent speakers usually notice this first loss?
options: They have nothing to compare it to
They never learned a second language
questions.explanation: 'Most fluent speakers never notice this loss; they have nothing to compare it to.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 2.

questions.prompt: What example does the writer give of a sentence a learner might produce that a native speaker would not?
options: 'The rain walks across the city like an old man.'
questions.explanation: 'The rain — it walks across the city like an old man.'

questions.prompt: Why does the writer say a native speaker rarely produces such metaphors?
options: They have learned what is and is not 'normal' to say, and the slight awkwardness that produced the metaphor has been smoothed away
Native speakers do not have imagination
Metaphors are illegal in some languages
questions.explanation: 'They have learned what is and is not "normal" to say, and the slight awkwardness that produced the original metaphor has been smoothed away.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer call fluency 'a kind of'?
questions.explanation: 'Fluency, in this respect, is a kind of polite censorship.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 3.

questions.prompt: Is the writer arguing against learning a second language?
options: No — the writer explicitly recognises the benefits and only asks us to notice what is also lost
questions.explanation: 'This is not an argument against learning. The benefits are obvious…' but 'competence costs something'.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say every native speaker is, 'in a sense'?
options: A former learner who has stopped noticing
questions.explanation: 'Every native speaker is, in a sense, a former learner who has stopped noticing.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of paragraph 4.

questions.prompt: What practical habit does the writer suggest for learners?
options: Keep a notebook of phrases that still feel strange, before fluency erases them
questions.explanation: 'Keep a small notebook of phrases that still feel strange. Write them down before fluency erases them.'

questions.prompt: Why does the writer suggest talking to a beginner?
options: To listen — their accidental, awkward sentences are sometimes the most accurate description of the world you will hear
questions.explanation: 'Not to correct them — to listen. Their accidental, awkward sentences are, occasionally, the most accurate description of the world…'

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean when he says the word has become 'transparent'?
options: We can read through paper now
We no longer notice the word itself — we see only the thing it refers to
The word is invisible to learners
The word has been deleted from the dictionary
questions.explanation: 'We see through it to the thing, instead of seeing it.'
questions.hint: Read the sentence right after 'has become transparent'.

questions.prompt: Which sentence best captures the writer's central claim?
options: Fluency is bad and should be avoided.
Fluency has hidden costs we should at least notice, even though it is also clearly valuable.
Children should not learn second languages.
questions.explanation: The whole essay holds two ideas together: fluency has real benefits, AND it quietly removes something.
questions.hint: Look at how the writer balances cost and benefit.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the writer NOT claiming?
options: That learners produce metaphors native speakers rarely reach for
That fluent speakers do not notice what they have lost
That bilingual children can retain some 'double hearing'
That we should stop learning languages to preserve our strangeness
questions.explanation: 'This is not an argument against learning.' The benefits are recognised.
questions.hint: Look for what the writer explicitly disclaims.

questions.prompt: What does 'competence costs something' mean in this essay?
options: Becoming skilled at the language quietly removes something — a sense of strangeness, a willingness to make unusual sentences
Competent speakers have to pay tax
questions.explanation: The sentence sums up the two losses the essay describes.
questions.hint: Read it in the context of paragraph 4.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's tone toward fluent native speakers?
options: Affectionate and respectful — but aware they have lost a way of seeing
questions.explanation: The writer calls them 'former learners who have stopped noticing' — without anger, but with awareness.
questions.hint: Look at the language about native speakers across the essay.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer suggests that fluent speakers have 'stopped noticing' what learners can still see in a language. Think of an area of your own life where you have become fluent — a job, a craft, a relationship. What did you used to notice when you were a beginner that has since gone transparent for you?
